description.narrative:
1.1) Availability of data on the number of persons being charged with particularly serious crimes, persons pardoned, and persons being executed (disaggregated by sex, age and crime). 1.2) Number of lawyers and other justice professionals (judges, prosecutors, court clerks, prison staff, etc.) participating in training programmes (disaggregated by sex). 1.3) Number of persons being accused/charged with particularly serious crimes (disaggregated by sex) receiving support. 1.4) Number of families with persons being accused/charged with particularly serious crimes receiving support (with data on family members disaggregated by sex).
2.1) Number of UNDP’s research studies on the right to a fair trial in particularly serious criminal cases. 2.2) Number of participating members of parliament, journalists, legal professionals, law students in training programmes, study missions, criminal moot court competitions, roundtables, etc. (disaggregated by sex). 2.3) Number of persons engaged in online and offline events (disaggregated by sex).

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

description.narrative: Mandatory Indicator 1.3.1: Area of sustainable management solutions at sub-national level for conservation of biodiversity and ecosystem services that benefit from integrated landscape and seascape planning and management approaches Mandatory Indicator 1.3.2: Number of households participating in improved and alternative livelihoods and sustainable resource management and best practice approaches Mandatory indicator 2.5.1: Extent to which Institutional frameworks are in place for integration of conservation, sustainable natural resource use, biodiversity and ecosystems and improved livelihoods into BR planning and management

comment.narrative: Approximately 0.367 million hectares (managed effectively) 0 (Baseline of households participating in improved and alternative livelihoods and sustainable resource management will be established through the commune/village microplanning process) Multiple use sustainable BR planning and management approaches absent or limited within the country

1. To initially establish the governance and coordination mechanism established at national level to support dialogue, information flow and decision–making between provinces and national levels for facilitating integrated planning and management of Biosphere Reserves 2. To initiate the revision process of legislation in support of integrated landscape planning and management of Biosphere Reserves 3. To prepare for development of legislation, technical guidelines, standards and norms for mainstreaming biodiversity conservation in natural resource use sectors in Biosphere Reserves
1. Completed the project implementation arrangments at national and provincial level. 2. Completed procurement processes to start various packages as planned 3. Project Inception mission completed

1. Revised legislation and technical guidelines for mainstreaming biodiversity conservation in natural resource use sectors and Biosphere Reserves management are developed. 2. Functional governance and coordination mechanism are established at national level to support dialogue, information flow and decision-making between provinces and national levels for facilitating integrated planning and management of Biosphere Reserves. 3. National strategy for BR management for 2021 – 2021 is developed (including action plan and BR advocacy strategy). 4. Multi-stakeholder and multi-sectoral coordination mechanism at Biosphere Reserve level to support integrated planning and management are established. 5. Assessment and mapping of biological, socio-economic, environmental and institutional aspects in 3 BRs are conducted. 6. Set-asides for at least 60,000ha. 4,000ha of forest restoration areas and priority communes for CCP activities are identified for implementation of conservation plans, forest restoration plans and CCPs in 3BRs. 7. Sustainable forest management plans (SFMP) and annual operation plans for six Pas are developed to submit for approval and implementation. 8. IBMA Plan through the approved of BR management plans of 3BRs are developed. 9. Biodiversity/ key endangered species monitoring plans in 3BRs are developed and implemented. 10. Investment plans to adjacent communities to reduce threats and improve sustainable resource use for set-aside areas in Dong Nai and Tay Nghe An BRs are developed for implementation. 11. Forest restoration and projection plans for degraded forest areas in 3BRs are developed for implementing. 12. Implementation of improved and alternative livelihoods, sustainable resource management and best practice approaches in 3BRs. 13. Ecotourism development guidelines, tourism resources evaluations and tourism development plans for 3BRs developed for implementation. 14. Training needs assessment, capacity building plan for BRs and training materials are conducted.

1. Revised legislation and technical guidelines for mainstreaming biodiversity conservation in natural resource use sectors and Biosphere Reserves management are developed. - The revised Law on Environmental Protection 2020 was adopted. - The decree for BR management, the BR strategy and the circular concerning guidance to implement some articles of revised Law on Environmental Protection 2020 have been developed for approval in Q1 2022. 2. Functional governance and coordination mechanism are established at national level to support dialogue, information flow and decision-making between provinces and national levels for facilitating integrated planning and management of Biosphere Reserves. The PMU was established at the central level and 3 Planning and Implementation Teams (PITs) were established at 3 sites for governance and coordination functions of the project. Besides, the cooperation and information sharing mechanism between BRs and related stakeholders are integrated in BR legal document, strategy and guidelines to improve the information flow and experience sharing between BRs and related stakeholders.
3. National strategy for BR management for 2021 – 2021 is developed (including action plan and BR advocacy strategy). National strategy for BR management has been developed for approval in Q1/2022 4. Multi-stakeholder and multi-sectoral coordination mechanism at Biosphere Reserve level to support integrated planning and management are established. The multi-stakeholder and multi-sectoral mechanism is prority approach to be integrated and applied for developing project outputs and implementing project activities. Those approaches are integrated and mainstreaming in dafts of BR legal document, BR strategy (period 2021-2030) and related technical guidelines (BR nomination and management guidelines, guideline to integrate biodiversity targets in provincial plan…), the 5. Assessment and mapping of biological, socio-economic, environmental and institutional aspects in 3 BRs are conducted.
The mapping processes were completed in Tay Nghe An BR from Q2 2021 and they are expected to be completed in Cu Lao Cham – Hoi An BR and Dong Nai BR in Q1 2022 due to covid-19’s impacts. All mapping outputs were developed and being revised for final approval.
6. Set-asides for at least 60,000ha. 4,000ha of forest restoration areas and priority communes for CCP activities are identified for implementation of conservation plans, forest restoration plans and CCPs in 3BRs.
- Completed: The mapping process identified total 63,698.30ha set-aside areas in three BRs (Dong Nai BR: 22,209ha; Cu Lao Cham - Hoi An BR: 731.66ha ; Tay Nghe An BR: 40,757.64ha). 4,489.34 ha of degraded forest in 3 BRs were identifed for restoration activities (including: Dong Nai BR: 2,212.23ha; Cu Lao Cham - Hoi An: 260ha; Tay Nghe An BR 2,017.11ha).
7. Sustainable forest management plans (SFMP) and annual operation plans for six Pas are developed to submit for approval and implementation. The sustainable forest management plans are developing for NPs/ NRs in Cu Lao Cham and Tay Nghe An BRs. The SFMPs of Cat Tien NP and Dong Nai NR were approved using co-financing budgets. Annual operation plans 2021 for six NPs/PAs are developing and revising for approval.
8. IBMA Plan through the approved of BR management plans of 3BRs are developed. The consultant firms were recruited for Dong Nai and Tay Nghe An BR from Dec 2021 (it is expected from Feb 2022 in Cu Lao Cham – Hoi An BR) to develop BR management plans that are integrated IBMA. Those BR Management plans are expected to be completed in Q3 2022.
9. Biodiversity/ key endangered species monitoring plans in 3BRs are developed and implemented. The biodiversity and targeted species monitoring was conducted in 3 BRs in 2021
10. Investment plans to adjacent communities to reduce threats and improve sustainable resource use for set-aside areas in Dong Nai and Tay Nghe An BRs are developed for implementation.
The mapping processes were completed in 3 BRs that identified a total of 63,698.30ha of set-aside areas in 3 BR (including 22,209ha in Dong Nai BR; 731.66ha in Cu Lao Cham – Hoi An BR; and 40,757.64ha in Tay Nghe An BR). The plans for non-exhausted use of natural resources for set-aside areas in 3 BRs are developing by the national consultantsfor implementing.
11. Forest restoration and protection plans for degraded forest areas in 3BRs are developed for implementing. The mapping process were completed in 3 BRs that identified 4,489.34 ha of degraded forest. The forest restoration and protection plans for degrated forest areas in 3 BRs are under developing and will be completed in Q2 2022.
12. Implementation of improved and alternative livelihoods, sustainable resource management and best practice approaches in 3BRs. 2 livelihood improvement projects have been approved by UNDP-GEF/SGP and signed contracts for implementation in Tay Nghe An BR. The implementation will commence in January 2022 with expected 996 household beneficiaries, among which 50% of direct beneficiaries are women. The implementation will last for 24 months. 13. Ecotourism development guidelines, tourism resources evaluations and tourism development plans for 3BRs developed for implementation. Ecotourism development guidelines for 3 BRs are developing and they are expected to be completed in Q2 and Q3 2022. Tourism resources evaluations are conducting in Dong Nai and Tay Nghe An BRs that will be completed in Q2 2022 (and it is expected to be conducted in Cu Lao Cham – Hoi An from Q1 2022). 14. Training needs assessment, capacity building plan for BRs and training materials are conducted. Consultants will be recruited to conduct training need assessment and develop capacity building plan for BRs from Q1 2022.

description.narrative: Extent to which legal or policy frameworks are in place for integration of socio-economic development and tourism into planning and management of Biosphere Reserves

comment.narrative: Specific, targeted Biosphere Reserve planning and management legislation largely lacking

Old: Revised legislation under Biodiversity Law/ Law on Environmental Protection and at least three legal instruments (decrees, circulars and guidelines) clarifying BR planning and management submitted to be adopted New: (i) Revised Law on Environmental Protection; (ii) Other instruments: 01 Decree Implementing the Law on Environmental Protection (iii) Guidelines clarifying BR nomination, planning and management submitted to be adopted

Accumulative progress to date: documents have been developed and approved, including Law on Environmental Protection (part on Biosphere Reserves); Decree 08/2022 (part on BRs); guidelines have been completed and submitted for adoption.

description.narrative: 1) One time-series tool called 'the Viet Nam Provincial Governance and Public Administration Performance Index (PAPI)' established to measure local government performance in Viet Nam. PAPI is a rigorous policy tool that monitors and measures the performance of governance and public administration of all 63 provinces in Viet Nam based on citizens’ experiences. 2) Number of provinces responding to PAPI findings by seeking measures to improve their performance over time 3) Number of in-depth research conducted to bring insights into why public polices do not work and how to make them work better for the benefit of citizens 4) Number of provinces where annual PAPI surveys are conducted 5) Number of respondents per cycle of PAPI surveys

comment.narrative: Baselines (2017) are: 1) The Viet Nam Provincial Governance and Public Administration Performance Index (PAPI) 2016 published and disseminated at the national level, involving more than 600 participants from different government and non-government sectors. 2016 PAPI findings also disseminated through 15 diagnostic workshops in 15 different provinces to inform public officials, civil servants and elected representatives about their provincial performance from citizen perspective. PAPI data and findings also shared in different mainstream media channels to promote policy discussion with provinces during 2017. 2) 38 provinces (cumulatively since 2011) having responded to PAPI findings by introducing provincial directives or action plans to improve their performance. 3) 31 case studies (cumulatively since 2011) using PAPI data to look into the supply side’s inputs for dimensions under PAPI study completed.

1) The Viet Nam Provincial Governance and Public Administration Performance Index (PAPI) 2019 published and disseminated at the national level, involving more than 500 participants from different government and non-government sectors. 2019 PAPI findings also disseminated through 15 diagnostic workshops in 15 different provinces to inform public officials, civil servants and elected representatives about their provincial performance from citizen perspective. PAPI data and findings also shared in different mainstream media channels to promote policy discussion with provinces during 2020. 2) 63 provinces (cumulatively since 2011) having responded to PAPI findings by introducing provincial directives or action plans to improve their performance. 3) 38 case studies (cumulatively since 2011) using PAPI data to look into the supply side’s inputs for dimensions under PAPI study completed. 4) 2020 PAPI surveys conducted in all 63 provinces, ready for data analysis and reporting in 2020. 5) 14,300 citizens from all 63 provinces responded to 2020 PAPI surveys

1) The Viet Nam Provincial Governance and Public Administration Performance Index (PAPI) 2019 published and disseminated virtually at the national level, involving nearly 1,000 direct viewers/participants from different government agencies, provinces and non-government stakeholders. 2019 PAPI findings also disseminated through 10 diagnostic workshops in 10 different provinces to inform public officials, civil servants and elected representatives about their provincial performance from citizen perspective. PAPI data and findings also shared in different mainstream media channels to promote policy discussion with provinces during 2020. 2) 63 provinces (cumulatively since 2011) having responded to PAPI findings by introducing provincial directives or action plans to improve their performance. 3) 38 case studies (cumulatively since 2011) using PAPI data to look into the supply side’s inputs for dimensions under PAPI study completed: due to COVID-19, activities under this result have been postponed to 2021. 4) 2020 PAPI surveys conducted in all 63 provinces, ready for data analysis and reporting from December 2020. 5) More than 15,500 citizens, including permanent residents and internal migrants, from all 63 provinces responded to 2020 PAPI surveys

1) The Viet Nam Provincial Governance and Public Administration Performance Index (PAPI) 2020 published and disseminated at the national level, involving more than 500 participants plus online participants from different government and non-government sectors. 2020 PAPI findings also disseminated through 15 diagnostic workshops in 15 different provinces to inform public officials, civil servants and elected representatives about their provincial performance from citizen perspective. PAPI data and findings also shared in different mainstream media channels to promote policy discussion with provinces during 2021. 2) 63 provinces (cumulatively since 2011) having responded to PAPI findings by introducing provincial directives or action plans to improve their performance. 3) 42 case studies (cumulatively since 2011) using PAPI data to look into the supply side’s inputs for dimensions under PAPI study completed. 4) 2021 PAPI surveys conducted in all 63 provinces, ready for data analysis and reporting in 2022. 5) 15,000 citizens including migrants from all 63 provinces responded to 2021 PAPI surveys 6) Citizen Powered Innovation Initiative criteria ready for selection of experimental provinces from Q4, 2021 7) Special research and advocacy knowledge products conducted and disseminated (feasibility of e-governance in 3 EM populated provinces; and mentoring of newly elected 2021-2026 Peoples Council members)

1) The 2020 PAPI Report was officially launched on 14 April 2021 in Ha Noi. More than 600 participants from a variety of central and provincial level agencies, national and international development partners, and embassies attended onsite and online. In particular, 136 participants who are provincial or sectoral leaders from 51 provinces came to attend the launch in person. The event was extensively covered on the media with large outreach.
The 2020 PAPI findings were also well disseminated through 13 diagnostic workshops with 13 different provinces both offline and online to inform public officials, civil servants and elected representatives about their provincial performance from citizen perspective. PAPI data and findings also shared in different mainstream media channels to promote policy discussion with provinces throughout 2021.
2) All 63 provinces (cumulatively since 2011) having responded to PAPI findings by introducing provincial directives or action plans to improve their performance.
3) 45 case studies (cumulatively since 2011) using PAPI data to look into the supply side’s inputs for dimensions under PAPI study completed.
4) 2021 PAPI surveys conducted in 54 provinces out of 63 provinces due to the COVID-19 impact. Nonetheless, surveys in the rest 9 provinces will be done January 2022, and data analysis and reporting will be done by April 2022 for launching.
5) As of 31 December 2021, about 12,550 citizens including migrants from 54 provinces responded to 2021 PAPI surveys.
6) Citizen Powered Innovation Initiative awardees decided (Tay Ninh and Vinh Phuc), ready for experimental innovation 2022
7) 5 thematic research and advocacy knowledge products conducted and disseminated (Roles of Women in Elected Bodies; E-government application in provinces with large ethnic minority population; Enhancing citizen access to land information; COVID 19 Impact on Governance and Public Administration from Citizens Perspective (Round 2); Implementation of SDG16 in Viet Nam since 2016 (SDG16+5). 2 other studies embarked on: Local governments’ performance in monitoring public health procurement and dispute resolution; and Action Research to Enhance Personal Data Privacy Practice in the Public Sector.

1) The Viet Nam Provincial Governance and Public Administration Performance Index (PAPI) 2021 published and disseminated at the national level, involving more than 500 participants on site plus online participants from different government and non-government sectors. 2) 2021 PAPI findings disseminated through more than 15 diagnostic workshops in 15 different provinces to inform public officials, civil servants and elected representatives about their provincial performance from citizen perspective. 3) 2021 PAPI data and findings shared in different mainstream media channels to promote policy discussion with provinces during 2022. 4) 63 provinces (cumulatively since 2011) having responded to PAPI findings by introducing/renewing provincial directives or action plans to improve their performance. 5) 48 case studies (cumulatively since 2011) using PAPI data to look into the supply side’s inputs for dimensions under PAPI study completed. 6) 2022 PAPI surveys conducted in all 63 provinces, ready for data analysis and reporting. 7) At least 15,000 citizens including migrants and persons with disabilities from all 63 provinces responded to 2022 PAPI surveys 8) Citizen Powered Innovation Initiative implemented in two selected provinces 9) Thematic research and advocacy knowledge products conducted and disseminated (feasibility of e-governance in 3 EM populated provinces; public procurement; citizen assessment of government responses; local governments’ performance in disclosure of about 2021 district land plans; protection of personal data on local governments’ online interfaces; disability inclusion in local governance and public administration) 10) Five training sessions provided for newly elected Peoples Council Deputies and persons with disabilities selected for training of potential candidates for 2026-2030 elections for elected bodies in Viet Nam

1) The Viet Nam Provincial Governance and Public Administration Performance Index (PAPI) 2021 officially launched on 10 May 2022 with at the national level, with the participation of more than 600 participants on site and online from different government and non-government sectors and 54 provinces. 2) 2021 PAPI findings disseminated through more than 31 diagnostic workshops in 31 different provinces to inform public officials, civil servants and elected representatives about their provincial performance from citizen perspective. 3) 2021 PAPI data and findings further discussed in a large number on different mainstream media channels to promote policy discussion with provinces during 2022 (see https://papi.org.vn/eng/category/thu-vien-en-eng/ for evidence) 4) All 63 provinces (cumulatively since 2011) having responded to PAPI findings by introducing/renewing provincial directives or action plans to improve their performance, while 44 provinces renewed their commitments in 2022. 5) 50 case studies (cumulatively since 2011) using PAPI data to look into the supply side’s inputs for dimensions under PAPI study completed (with 6 provinces with large ethnic minority population and Tay Ninh and Ha Noi included in 2022). 6) 2022 PAPI surveys conducted in all 63 provinces by 1 Dec, ready for data analysis and reporting from December 2022. 7) A record number of 16,117 citizens including migrants and persons with disabilities from all 63 provinces responded to 2022 PAPI surveys 8) Citizen Powered Innovation Initiative implemented in two selected provinces, including Tay Ninh and Ha Giang 9) 14 policy and action research papers and advocacy products in both English and Vietnamese, 8 report launches and thematic discussions, and 6 published knowledge products in English, covering PAPI’s 8 focused areas in 2022, including: (1) national and provincial performance in governance and public administration (the 2021 PAPI Report and written articles); (2) citizen assessment of government responses to COVID-19 pandemic in 2021; (3) businesses’ assessment of public health procurement during the second COVID-19 year of 2021; (4) local governments’ performance in disclosure of about 2021 district land plans and 2020-2024 provincial land price frames through the Land Transparency Initiative; (5) assessment of access to e-services for ethnic minority communities; (6) protection of personal data on local governments’ online interfaces; (7) disability inclusion in local governance and public administration from perception and experience of the persons with disabilities; and, (8) citizen participation in formulation and implementation of the Law on Grassroots Democracy Implementation. 10) Two (02) training sessions provided for newly elected Peoples Council Deputies and three (03) training and recruitment sessions to select persons with disabilities training/mentoring of potential candidates for 2026-2030 elections for elected bodies in Viet Nam

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

comment.narrative: 1. No sustainable development solutions identified, experimented and documented within AccLab 2. No blogs from Viet Nam Lab to share with a public audience (outside of UNDP) 3. No partners and governments take up of the innovation methods 4. No network effects among the Accelerator Labs

1. 5 sustainable development solutions identified, experimented and documented 2. At least 6 blogs or other (substantial) media posts published per year 3. At least one government entity or partner has adopted an accelerator lab method 4. The lab interacts at least once a week with another lab in the network. 48 interaction per year

1. 19 sustainable development solutions identified, experimented and documented (14 in EPPIC and 5 in Danang CE Hub) 2. 5 blogs published 3. Da Nang Institute of Economic and Social Development adopts the Accelerator Lab method 4. The lab interacts with other labs in regular drop-in calls and separate interactions with other labs work on the same topics as waste, plastic waste, informal sector, collective intelligence, citylabs, circular economy

1. 10 sustainable development solutions identified, experimented and documented 2. At least 8 blogs or other (substantial) media posts published per year 3. 1. 40 sustainable development solutions identified, experimented and documented (3 in EPPIC and 37 in National CE Hub) 2. 8 blogs and 1 paper on a magazine 3. 6 national partners adopts the accelerator lab method ( National Innovation Center, National economic university, Foreign trade university, Danang Incubator DNES, DISED) 4. The lab interacts with other labs in regular drop-in calls and separate interactions with other labs work on the same topics as waste, plastic waste, informal sector, collective intelligence, citylabs, circular economy, SMEs

The Conference has been postponed due to COVID-situation.
01 conference with 3 parallel events At least 400 participants At least 48 countries At least 200 media coverages by international and national media

01 Conference was organised, with 3 parallel events, 2 side events and 20 exhibition booths.
The Conference was opened by H.E. Le Van Thanh, Deputy Prime Minister of Viet Nam and H.E. Anniken Huitfeldt, Minister of Foreign Affairs of Norway, who provided a virtual opening speech. The Conference was co-chaired by H.E. Le Minh Ngan, Deputy Minister of Natural Resources and Environment of Viet Nam and H.E. Bjørg Sandkjær, State Secretary of the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Norway.
More than 400 people joined in person at the venue in Hanoi, Viet Nam, and a further 1,000 participated online from around the world through the virtual Conference platform. The livestream broadcasts of key Conference sessions received more than 50,000 views.
The Conference was attended by Ministers or their senior representatives from Foreign Affairs, Environment, Natural Resources, Climate Change, Ocean and Marine Resources, Agriculture and Fisheries, Sustainable Development, Planning, Tourism and related sectors from 44 countries, including Small Island Developing States and other climate vulnerable coastal countries from the Pacific and across Asia, to Africa, Latin America and the Caribbean, as well as representatives from international organizations and finance institutions, international and national research institutions, thinktanks and global centres of excellence, social and non-governmental organizations, and the media.
Across the two days of the Conference, ministers and senior officials from 18 countries chaired sessions and addressed the conference, including from Bangladesh, Canada, Cuba, Fiji, France, Germany, Ghana, Haiti, Honduras, Indonesia, Japan, Kenya, Lao PDR, Federated States of Micronesia, Norway, the Philippines, Samoa and Viet Nam. Senior representatives and experts of 11 United Nations agencies, international organizations and academic institutions also addressed the Conference, including the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), and the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), the Intergovernmental Oceanographic Commission (IOC) of UNESCO, the Asian Development Bank (ADB), the International Federation of Red Cross And Red Crescent Societies (IFRC), the International Renewable Energy Agency (IRENA), the Norwegian Geotechnical Institute (NGI), the South Pacific Tourism Organization in Fiji (SPTO), the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI), and the Faculty of Fisheries and Marine Sciences/Center for Coastal and Marine Resources Studies at the IPB University in Indonesia.
The Conference had extensive media coverage with 235 articles and 29 TV news reports, including Viet Nam’s prime time national TV news. Social media channels reached 76,500 people. Livestream broadcasts received more than 50,000 views.

description.narrative: # Number of supporting facilities functioned to support private sector business in investing in Agriculture NDC measures # Number of trainings introduced to support businesses in development of pipeline investment projects toward climate smart and green businesses # Number of business models in the supply chains for Agriculture get supported by NDC supporting facilities # Number of firms in agriculture supply chains get access to finance and supporting facilities from MPI SME Fund # Number of Data Hub established to support SME in green supply chain # Number of Private Sector Advisory Network established link with NDC IP # Investment Forum for NDC as part of NDC IP # Number of firms registered climate actions # Transparency platform established for large emitter firms/supply chains, particularly those who are highly consuming the energy and environment resources

comment.narrative: # No supporting facilities functioned to support private sector business in investing in Agriculture NDC measures # Businesses are lack of capacity to development of pipeline investment projects toward climate smart and green businesses # Not available model proven as green supply chains for Agriculture as part of NDC # Business lack of access or have limit funding for green business or climate actions, including lack of financial and technical supporting facilities from Government # Lack of data to support private sector in taking decision for climate action/green supply chain # No Private Sector Advisory Network established # Limit Forum for private sector to participate into NDC Implementation platform # No platform to track firms in taking their climate actions # No transparency mechanism established for large emitter firms/supply chains, particularly those who are highly consuming the energy and environment resources

- 01 Number of supporting facilities functioned to support private sector business in investing in Agriculture NDC measures - 01 Number of trainings introduced to support businesses in development of pipeline investment projects toward climate smart and green businesses - 01 Private Sector Advisory Network established link with NDC IP

- 01 supporting facility to promote Innovative approaches to engage cooperatives and farmer groups in green solutions are promoted explored via green shrimp contests and application of livestreaming in promotion for green products to high end domestic markets - 01 supply chain mapping and roadmap for green agriculture commodities undertaken. This is complimented by the recommendation from the project to establish an integrated Agriculture Data Hub to tackle the shortcomings of quality, availability and transparency of agriculture data for greening agriculture commodities - 01 meeting of Private Sector Advisory Network established link with NDC IP organised by UNDP-MARD on “Accelerating Agriculture Business Towards Low-Emission and Climate-Resilient Pathways in Viet Nam”. Participants were from MARD ICD, PSAV, Crop Department, Binh Thuan People Committee, Ham Thuan Nam Districts, Binh Thuan Agriculture Department, TE-Food, Lavifood (leading fruit processing firms), Farmers from Cooperatives of Dragon fruits, IFC representative, SNV, etc

1 - 01 supporting facilities functioned to support private sector business in investing in Agriculture NDC measures 2 - 02 Number of trainings introduced to support businesses in development of pipeline investment projects toward climate smart and green businesses (EU + BMU) 3 - 02 Number of business models in the supply chains for Agriculture get supported by NDC supporting facilities 4 - 01 firms in agriculture supply chains get access to finance and supporting facilities from MPI SME Fund 5 - n/a 6 - 01 Private Sector Advisory Network established link with NDC IP 7 - 01 Investment Forum for NDC as part of NDC IP 8 - n/a - 00 Number of firms registered climate actions (EU) 9 - n/a Transparency platform established for large emitter firms/supply chains, particularly those who are highly consuming the energy and environment resources

1. 01 supporting facilities functioned to support private sector business in investing in Agriculture NDC measures - Completed/on track, 01 in placed and 01 finalized. - 01 project facility functioned in two provinces on providing green technology practices of renewable energy or energy efficient, water saving and green processing chains for agriculture productions; - 01 financial facility on direct lending support regulation/policy are being finalized by MPI. The two facilities are to support private sector business in investing in Agriculture NDC measures
2. Number of trainings introduced to support businesses in development of pipeline investment projects toward climate smart and green businesses Completed/On track: - 02 orientation/training was provided online to Provincial Cooperatives in Binh Thuan and Bac Lieu to provide orientation on green business operation; green e-commerce with some piloting - 02 Technical workshops with local businesses and cooperatives in Binh Thuan in April and Bac Lieu in Aug (on-line) to discussed on potential business cooperation was organized and recommendation on future training to strengthen the business in 2022 - 02 technical training on green model development for eco-shrimp and mangrove models in Bac Lieu for 60 farmers (20% is female participants)
3. 02 business models in the supply chains for Agriculture get supported On track with some adjusted progress: - 03 dragon fruit model/cooperatives get supported for livestreaming to promote green and global Gap fruits to domestic markets - 01 business model on sustainable low carbon shrimp rice production toward organic practice supported. The model was the best qualified for support from the green shrimp contest; - 04 more shrimp models were selected for project support but pending operation due to COVID19 impacts - 03 Green dragon fruit cooperatives/models using low carbon and resilient farming technologies were identified but the operation was delayed due to Covid 19 impacts and are ready to operate soonest
4. 01 firm in agriculture supply chains get access to finance and supporting facilities from MPI SME Fund On track with adjusted progress: - There was 01 cooperative/model for Bac Lieu green eco shrimp-rice farming with co-funding from local cooperative members to operationalize their initiative in 2021 (of 1.5billion VND co-finance) - The regulation for SMEDF directly funding with green investments was drafted but not yet approved. Funding therefore was delayed for operation.
6. 01 Private Sector Advisory Network established link with NDC IP Progress with adjustment of COVID context and impacts - 01 consultation workshop on roles of private sector in NDC and Viet Nam NCCS with line ministries and medium/large scale businesses in Viet Nam in Dec. This is a part of projects support to the NCCS development to implement Viet Nam NDC, the project support to assess the key bottlenecks and opportunities for private sector engagement in the NDC. Base on the report, a roadmap and a network of private sector for NDC in Viet Nam might be initiated in fuller potential
7. 01 Investment Forum for NDC as part of NDC Implementation Plan Delayed. The activity is delayed by MARD due Government extended process for NDC implementation planning and NCCS finalization
9. (not yet) - Transparency platform established for large emitter firms/supply chains, particularly those who are highly consuming the energy and environment resources

1. 02 supporting facilities functioned to support private sector business in investing in Agriculture NDC measures 2. At least 4 trainings on various topics for pipeline investment projects toward climate smart and green businesses, green business models in the supply chains for Agriculture available, standardized supported 3. At least 4 new green models are improved with strengthened linkages across the supply chain with green business performance 4. A direct lending regulation in place for demonstration at the provinces with cooperation of MARD-UNDP and SMEDF; At least 01 model get pilot seed funding from SMEDF or similar fund for co-investment with project seed funding 5. At least 01 datahub established in Binh Thuan or Bac Lieu for demonstration 6. A TOR on Private Sector Working Group/Network is developed with potential functioned under the NCCS/NDC implementation mechanism hosted by MONRE or initiated by MARD 7. At least 01 business forum will be organized with HL ministerial level chairman 8. Completed - n/a 9. Transparency platform established for large emitter firms/supply chains, particularly those who are highly consuming the energy and environment resources: A clear approach for calculating carbon footprint developed and piloted in the province for the shrimp or dragon fruit supply chain

condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document

Consistent with the Article III of the SBAA [or the Supplemental Provisions to the Project Document], the responsibility for the safety and security of the Implementing Partner and its personnel and property, and of UNDP’s property in the Implementing Partner’s custody, rests with the Implementing Partner. To this end, the Implementing Partner shall: a) put in place an appropriate security plan and maintain the security plan, taking into account the security situation in the country where the project is being carried; b) assume all risks and liabilities related to the Implementing Partner’s security, and the full implementation of the security plan.

Social and environmental sustainability will be enhanced through application of the UNDP Social and Environmental Standards (http://www.undp.org/ses) and related Accountability Mechanism (http://www.undp.org/secu-srm).

The project aims to accelerate the SDGs and implementation of climate ambitions by supporting a cooperation platform, identifying new initiatives and scaling up public investment and performance on the SDGs and net-zero emissions. The proposed initiative, aims to further: (i) Initiatives that allow CO to accelerate the common chapter agenda in the UNDP Strategic Plan, behind joint results with UN partners; (ii) Enhancement of Country Programme and Strategic Plan outcomes in this recovery-to-sustainability period, through higher levels of policy ambition; (iii) Addressing of corporate and regional priority issues of scaling innovation, including through digital access, gender equality, acceleration to carbon neutrality, SDG finance; and (iv) Leverage the catalytic potential of these resources with a high rate of return on investment (expected target of 1:20).

title.narrative: Programme development and policy advising on economic growth and sustainable poverty reduction

description.narrative: The objective of the initiation plan is to formulate innovative projects to support Viet Nam in preparing and implementing policies that respond to the requirements of UNDP’s new Country Program Document (2022-2026) with emerging risks in the poly-crisis context: global economic recession, war in Ukraine, imminent impacts of climate change. By engaging partners in (i) conducting policy research, technical consultation and providing policy advice, (ii) policy dialogues to identify new areas of UNDP interventions to support an equitable and sustainable economic growth that is resilient to climate change impacts, and (iii) consultative meetings and stakeholder engagements experimentation to identify new solutions, design and implement new programmes, partnerships and resource mobilization opportunities to meet the Government’s priorities in line with UNDP’s CPD. Key expected products will be strategic policy studies and advisory notes/papers, new project proposals/concepts submitted to potential partners, UNDP

title.narrative: Supporting the implementation of net-zero nature-positive (NZNP) solutions in Viet Nam

description.narrative: The objective of the GEF PPG is to develop the project concept into a full project: Supporting the implementation of net-zero nature-positive (NZNP) solutions in Viet Nam. As described in the project concept (PIF/child project concept note), this project aims to support Viet Nam to enhance the implementation of NZNP solutions that reduces Greenhouse Gases (GHG) emissions (mainly methane emissions), while at the same time, bring biodiversity and land conservation co-benefits, both at the national level and in select sectors (AFOLU Sector-rice cultivation, animal husbandry and waste management).

The Strengthening Viet Nam’s One Health Implementation at the Nexus of Climate Change and Environment (VOHICCE) project aims to support the Government of Viet Nam (GVN) to improve national and provincial capacities to detect, prevent, and respond to climate change related emerging infectious diseases in Viet Nam, and to strengthen One Health Coordination and One Health Workforce strategies implementation. The project will focus its technical, policy and coordination support efforts at both the national level, working with the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development (MARD), the Ministry of Health (MOH), and the Ministry of Natural Resources and Environment (MONRE), and the provincial level, specifically in selected Mekong Delta locations including An Giang province, Can Tho city and Soc Trang province. title.narrative: Telehealth to improve access to Healthcare in VN

description.narrative: The project goals are to promote the health of disadvantaged groups with logistical limits by offering good quality healthcare services to ethnic minorities, people with disabilities and others living in mountainous and remote areas of Viet Nam and will strengthen digital transformation in health services contributing to improving the quality of grassroots medical services. Support will be provided to strengthen the standard operating procedures (SOPs) and technical documents for telehealth/telemedicine through the development and dissemination of SOPs and other technical documents and guidelines. The capacities of the grassroots telehealth system will be strengthened through upgrading the ‘Doctor for Everyone’ software, improving the IT infrastructure of the health sector; setting up the telehealth function rooms at the grassroots health facilities in selected provinces; and providing training to healthcare personnel in selected provinces on the grassroots telehealth system using the ‘Doctor for Everyone’ so

description.narrative: This initiation plan will deliver activities that contribute to the implementation and development of further pipeline projects under the outputs defined under 2022 – 2026 UNDP Viet Nam’s Country Programme Document’s Outcome 3, which aims at supporting Viet Nam in building a more just, safe, and inclusive society based on improved governance, more responsive institutions, strengthened rule of law and the protection of and respect for human rights, gender equality and freedom from all forms of violence and discrimination in line with international standards. In particular, the plan focuses on supporting Viet Nam to: (1) strengthen its implementation of international human rights recommendations received and accepted through UN Human Rights Mechanisms (both Charter and Treaty based); (2) advance human rights education and training, and (3) promote responsible business and sustainable consumption in alignment with UNPGs, and (4) strengthen participatory and human-rights based approach towards the formulation of the new engagement in mine action and resilient rural development.

title.narrative: PIMS-10094 Umbrella Programme to Support NBSAP Update and the 7th National Reports Viet Nam"

description.narrative: This Global Programme will support countries in revising and updating theirNational Biodiversity Strategies and Action Plans (NBSAPs) in order to align with the recently agreedupon Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, and enable effective implementation of theConvention on Biological Diversity (CBD) and the Kunming Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework atthe national level. In addition, this grant supports countries in preparing and submitting their 7thDocuSign Envelope ID: 8FA89947-0441-46F1-B6AA-6BD39D3C7EC22 | P a g eNational Report on the implementation of the Convention on Biological Diversity and the KunmingMontreal Global Biodiversity Framework.This Global Programme will be responsive to CBD guidance on developing NBSAPs and completing the7th National Report, including recent COP15 outcomes and decisions. It will also build upon existingwork through the Global Biodiversity Framework Early Action Support (GBF-EAS) that is ongoing, aswell as support to countries to develop biodiversity finance plans, through the recently approved“Umbrella Programme to Support Development of Biodiversity Finance Plans”. The projectimplementation units of all three initiatives will coordinate to ensure that resources from bothprojects are used effectively and efficiently towards the update or revision of the NationalBiodiversity Strategies and Action Plans and the preparation of the 7th National Reports in thetargeted countries.

title.narrative: PIMS-9835 Reduce waste and chemicals of concerns through promoting circularity in electric and electronic management in Viet Nam (PPG)

description.narrative: The objective of the GEF PPG is to develop the project concept into a full project: Reduce waste and chemicals of concerns through promoting circularity in electric and electronic management in Viet Nam. As described in the child project concept note, this project aims to significantly reduce the production of electronic waste and increase circularity and resource recovery in the electronics sector of the country, through the creation of an enabling environment, including access to finance, technology and policy and legislative change that fosters responsible electronics management.

title.narrative: The Biodiversity Finance Initiative – Phase II (BIOFIN Vietnam)

description.narrative: The Biodiversity Finance Initiative – BIOFIN – is a global programme that developed, piloted, and is continuously improving a methodology to measure existing biodiversity expenditure levels, assess future financial needs, and design comprehensive plans to use finance tools and solutions that increase financing, effectiveness and efficiency of biodiversity management in up to 132 countries. BIOFIN’s first phase (2012-2018) enabled full assessments and initial implementation of finance plan elements in target countries. Significant scaling up of finance solutions is required in all countries to address the biodiversity finance challenge.

description.narrative: As part of a Joint-SDG Programme with FAO and IFAD, the initiation plan is developed for UNDP’s interventions in this Joint Programme (JP). It aims to explore entry points and cooperation opportunities for mobilization of greater support from international communities, which will be seeded with at least one project concept note through initial administrative and technical support to the establishment and operation of Food Systems Transformation Partnership (FSTP) and its working groups, especially regarding considerations of climate impacts on the food systems. Gender equality and women’s empowerment will be integrated in the whole process.

title.narrative: Catalysing Lower Emissions through Advancing National Adoption of Commercially – Viable Technologies (CLEAN-ACT)

description.narrative: In support of these endeavours, a consortium of six partners will join forces to implement the ‘Shifting Investment Flows Towards Green Transformation (SHIFT)’ project in Viet Nam, enhancing know-how and capacities of relevant public and private stakeholders in the areas of green finance and energy transition. The consortium consists of Agora Energiewende/the Smart Energy for Europe Platform (SEFEP), the Banking Academy of Viet Nam (BAV), International Finance Cooperation (IFC), SNV Netherlands Development Organisation Viet Nam, the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Internationale Zusammenarbeit (GIZ) as the consortium lead. Within the framework of SHIFT, UNDP is responsible for the design and implementation of two Work Packages. The objective is to generate interest and build technical capacity in the private sector especially women-led enterprises or enterprises with a proven approach to promote gender equality concerning RE, EE and energy transition technologies. UNDP will receive 3 million Euros (of 15 million Euros for the entire project) to implement the following two work packages: (1) design and implementation of replicable investment projects in energy transition technologies to substantially reduce greenhouse gas emissions in high energy consuming sectors; and (2) catalyse and facilitate the replication and scale-up of the piloted energy transition technologies and other energy transition technologies.

title.narrative: Emergency Response and Recovery for Typhoon Yagi 2024

description.narrative: Typhoon Yagi, one of the strongest storms over three decades with great intensity and wide range winds has stormed the Northern part of Vietnam in September 2024. The typhoon has left a destructive trail and continues to affect the lives and livelihoods of millions across northern regions. Dealing with the aftermath of the typhoon, the emergency relief to disaster is fundamental and necessary for millions of people to recover and build resilience in the aftermath of the Typhoon. On 9 September 2024, the Government of Viet Nam formally requested support from the international community to assist in recovery and rebuilding efforts. Given UNDP’s longstanding role in providing comprehensive disaster and recovery assistance to Viet Nam, our immediate intervention is crucial. In response to the Government of Viet Nam’s request for support, UNDP and other contributors (for example: Embassy of Republic of Korea (ROK), etc.) are collaborating on a new recovery initiation plan. This initiation plan will adopt and follow with UNDP’s post-disaster strategy, ‘Building Back Better for Resilience, Inclusive Growth, and Empowerment’ (BRIDGE), encompasses activities across the post-disaster recovery continuum. Building Back Better guides UNDP’s approach to disaster recovery to strengthen the long-term resilience of people, natural ecosystems, and infrastructure, ensuring that affected populations emerge from crises and are resilient enough to withstand future shocks. This approach enables communities, local mass organisations, and the Government to reduce their exposure to hazards while seizing new opportunities for resilience.

The Project supports Viet Nam in addressing its plastic problem through several key interventions. The National Plastic Action Partnership (NPAP) platform will facilitate stakeholder engagement, knowledge sharing, and innovation scaling to promote plastic action. A scheme will be implemented across three fishing ports to encourage 500 fishing boats and 2,500 fishermen to bring plastic waste back to shore for collection and recycling, aiming to collect 7,000 tons of plastic waste in three years. The initiative also includes piloting harmonized responsible sourcing to improve the working conditions and livelihoods of 750 informal waste workers, conducting national and regional dialogues on best practices, and supporting six innovative local solutions to plastic pollution. Finally, technical guidelines and behavior change campaigns in Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh City will support the implementation of mandatory waste sorting at source, with at least ten districts in Hanoi and seven in Ho Chi Minh City participating, and an expected 10,000 tons of recyclables directed to material recovery facilities.

title.narrative: Programme development and policy advising on economic growth and sustainable poverty reduction including response to shocks

description.narrative: The Initiation Plan will (i) foster research ideas on human-centred policy options, including but not limited to inclusive and equitable access to economic development opportunities, social services and benefits, digital transformation, and renewable energy; (ii) engage government officials, academic institutions, the business community and civil society in inclusive discussions of economic affairs, socio-economic development policies and national development plans and strategies to help build a national consensus in support of a development process that leaves no one behind; (iii) identify gaps in programming via stakeholder consultations to design and implement new programmes on inclusive and sustainable growth, (iv) cash assistance to communities and technical assistance to local businesses which have been affected by the typhoon in Ha Giang.

title.narrative: Gender Equality and Women’s Empowerment project (GEWE)

description.narrative: This Project Initiation Plan (PIP) aims at implementing several seeding initiatives during the critical period of January 2025 to June 2026 to help Viet Nam to achieve its national targets on women’s political participation. These initiatives are also designed to consolidate all UNDP’s efforts and strengthen the partnerships with national counterparts and development partners, that will enable UNDP to deliver more comprehensive and sustainable offers on promoting women’s political participation as well as the women, peace, and security agenda in Viet Nam.The two key interventions are to strengthen women’s political participation in elected bodies and enhance women’s meaningful participation in international and national peace and security efforts. To achieve these goals, the project will bolster national institutional capacity by supporting the Training Center for Elected Representatives (TCER) of the National Assembly to design and deliver gender-inclusive training modules. This support will enable TCER to provide specialized training for women deputies, empowering them to meaningfully engage in policy discussions, particularly in critical areas such as energy. Additionally, the project will serve as a strategic entry point for UNDP to support the Ministry of Defense in the implementation of the National Action Plan on Women, Peace and Security (NAP WPS) and will be catalytic to the elevation of their existing partnership. It will create the space, trust, and mechanisms for the longer-term provision of technical expertise and capacity-building on women empowerment, gender equality and evidence-based reforms, which will further enable the defence sector to advance the WPS agenda. The initiative aligns with UNDP’s broader efforts to promote women’s political participation and inclusive climate governance in Viet Nam. The project activities have been developed by the Country Office in close consultation with national counterparts. The PIP is fully aligned also wit

description.narrative: Building on the key achievements of Phase 1, UNJP Vietnam, implemented by UNDP, UNFPA, UNICEF, aims in Phase 2 to further promote disability inclusion in two critical systems essential to the well-being of persons with disabilities (PwDs): the education system and the sexual and reproductive health (SRH) service delivery system. The UNJP will support the Government of Vietnam (GoV) including the Ministry of Education and Training (MOET), the Ministry of Labour, Invalids and Social Affairs (MOLISA), the Ministry of Health (MOH), organizations of persons with disabilities (OPDs) in developing more comprehensive systems on inclusive education and SRH. These systems will be piloted in two provinces, with a strategy to scale up effectively, ensuring the continued implementation and quality of prioritized services beyond the programmes conclusion.

title.narrative: Accelerating renewable energy development and strengthening climate resilience for enhanced human security and sustainable growth in Viet Nam

description.narrative: Viet Nams rapidly expanding economy has historically depended on fossil fuels, positioning it among the fastest-growing per-capita greenhouse gas emitters and placing it within the top twenty most coal-dependent nations globally. At COP26, Viet Nam committed to achieving net-zero emissions by 2050 and endorsed the Global Coal to Clean Power Transition Statement.To support these ambitious goals, on December 14, 2022, the Government of Viet Nam and the International Partners Group, which includes Japan, established the Just Energy Transition Partnership (JETP). This partnership aims to foster a long-term collaboration to facilitate Viet Nams shift toward low-emission, climate-resilient development. In addition, on November 19, 2024, the Prime Minister approved an updated National Climate Change Adaptation Plan for the 2021–2030 period, with a vision extending to 2050. This plan emphasizes enhancing the resilience and adaptive capacity of natural, economic, and social systems to ensure sustainable livelihoods; reducing risks, damages, and climate-related losses from disasters and extreme events; and strengthening institutional frameworks and mobilising national resources for effective adaptation.The proposed project is designed to accelerate renewable energy development in Viet Nam, focusing on offshore wind and hybrid solar-battery systems. It aims to promote a just, inclusive, and sustainable energy transition while scaling up effective models for climate change adaptation, disaster risk reduction and preparedness, and resilience-building initiatives. The initiative is expected to directly benefit approximately 2,836 individuals, half of whom are women, and indirectly impact around 64,000 people, with women also representing 50 percent of this group. Over a 20-year period (i.e., the lifetime of the solar system), the project is projected to avert 92 metric tons of CO2 emissions annually and eliminate the need for 1,111 tons of coal.

The initiation plan aims to mobilize resources through the formulation of new projects and finalization of project concept notes and proposals in inclusive governance, with a focus on access to justice, rule of law, and anti-corruption. The plan also prioritizes strategic policy analysis and stakeholder consultations to strengthen inclusive governance, with a focus on the rule of law, enhancing access to justice, and combating corruption. It will emphasize innovative, rights-based approaches that promote transparency, accountability, and equitable policy and legal frameworks, with a key focus on ensuring gender equality and the empowerment of marginalized groups. The plan will deliver high-quality project proposals and concept notes tailored for major funding opportunities, such as the the European Union (EU), the Bureau of International Narcotics and Law Enforcement Affairs (INL), the Global Programme, Norway, SIDA, and others. Additionally, it will generate strategic studies, policy recommendations, and expert-driven publications to inform decision-making and foster impactful policy dialogues that can in turn lead to resource mobilization.

title.narrative: CCE_ (IFCEE) Integrated Framework for Climate Change, Energy and Environment Initiatives

description.narrative: The project aims to address key development challenges associated with climate change, energy transition, environmental degradation, and climate-induced health issues in Viet Nam. As the country faces increasing climate-related risks, such as extreme weather events, rising temperatures, and environmental degradation, there is an urgent need to enhance resilience, reduce emissions, and foster sustainable development aligned with the Country Programme Document’s 2022-2026 Outcome 2 and signature solution on crisis prevention and increased resilience. This project is to consolidate and manage a set of pipelines to materialize funding opportunities on climate change, energy and environment initiatives (CCEE), aiming to mobilize resources and improve performance for these following portfolios: i) Enhance resilience to climate change and disasters; ii) Promote green and low-carbon development; iii) Circular economy and waste/plastic; iv) Foster biodiversity conservation and ecosystem restoration; and v) Strengthen the climate-health nexus. With the objectives of resources mobilization for CCEE unit, this project will contribute to:? Enhance programme development on climate change and environmental topics, catalyze national efforts for sustainability, and support Viet Nam in achieving its climate and environmental goals while promoting green, inclusive development.? Provide top-notch policy advice and quality assurance to ensure that climate-vulnerable communities and marginalized groups benefit from innovative climate and environmental initiatives.

title.narrative: Strengthening Women’s Political Participation in Viet Nam’s Elected Bodies

description.narrative: The project aims to bolster national institutional capacity to promote women in Viet Nam’s elected bodies, accompany elected women in building their public policy, leadership and lawmaking skills, and influence public opinion in the leadup to the critical juncture of the 2026 elections. Across these areas, the project will create opportunities for national partners to learn from Canada’s experience in advancing the representation of women in national and subnational governance. Such efforts will help advance Viet Nam’s recently adopted Women, Peace and Security National Action Plan, which is also benefiting from Canada’s strong support. By contributing to greater and more meaningful women’s participation in Viet Nam’s elected bodies, the project aims to support long-term, inclusive sustainable development. Based on ample global evidence as well as evidence from Vietnam, the project’s underlying assumption – which aligns with that of Canada’s FIAP - is that women in leadership roles contribute to policies that enhance economic opportunities for women, improve public expenditure on child and maternal health, education, and social protection. Together, such policy outcomes have been linked to reduced poverty and socio-economic inclusion, including in Viet Nam.

title.narrative: Enhancing capacity and meaningful engagements in UN human rights mechanisms

description.narrative: The overall goal of the project is to enhance the national capacity to engage effectively and meaningfully with UN human rights mechanisms. The project aligns with the “Human Rights 75 Initiative” (2023), which aims to reinforce the global human rights ecosystem, including both charter-based and treaty-based bodies, to advance the protection and promotion of human rights worldwide. Project interventions will focus on strengthening the technical capacity of government agencies to improve their reporting, monitoring, and implementation of international human rights obligations. This includes building the capacity of government actors to produce reliable, evidence-based reports and engage effectively with treaty body reviews. More importantly, the project will support the government in conducting meaningful and broad consultation workshops with relevant stakeholders on national periodic reports to treaty bodies or the Universal Periodic Review (UPR) mechanism.

title.narrative: EU Justice and Legal Empowerment Programme - Phase II (EU JULE II)

description.narrative: The EU Justice and Legal Empowerment Programme Phase II (EU JULE II) seeks to strengthen Viet Nam’s rule of law and human rights systems in line with international standards and national frameworks by building institutional capacity, reforming policies and laws, and promoting digital transformation in the justice sector. Its core aim is to make justice institutions inclusive, transparent, and accountable, ensuring equal access to justice for all, particularly vulnerable and marginalized groups. The programme supports SDG 16 by improving the effectiveness and accessibility of the justice system, SDG 5 by embedding gender-responsive approaches in legal reform and service delivery, and SDG 10 by addressing systemic barriers to justice for disadvantaged groups. Key interventions include capacity building, legal and policy reform, enhanced coordination among justice and human rights institutions, and public awareness campaigns on human rights and legal remedies, with a strong focus on inclusion and equality.

title.narrative: Digital Transformation of Adverse Drug Reaction (ADR) Reporting and Pharmacovigilance System in Viet Nam

description.narrative: This five-month project will initiate the digital transformation of Viet Nam’s pharmacovigilance system by developing the core elements of an international standards-compliant platform for adverse drug reaction (ADR) reporting and analysis at the National Drug Information & ADR Monitoring Centre. In partnership with WHO and the Ministry of Health, UNDP will map current workflows, design a secure data standards-aligned database, migrate and standardise legacy ADR data, and pilot a functional prototype supported by staff training and system documentation.

title.narrative: Emergency Response and Recovery for the 2025 Typhoons and Floods

description.narrative: During 2025, Viet Nam experienced a series of severe and compounding climate-related disasters, including Typhoons Bualoi, Matmo, Fengshen, and Kalmaegi, which triggered widespread flooding, landslides, and extensive damage to housing, infrastructure, livelihoods, and essential services across multiple regions. The cumulative impacts of these successive events disproportionately affected vulnerable populations, particularly in the Central Highlands and Northern mountainous provinces, including Gia Lai, Dak Lak, Da Nang, Cao Bang, Lang Son, Tuyen Quang, Phu Tho, Bac Ninh and Thai Nguyen provinces, where ethnic minority communities, poor and near-poor households, women-headed households, older persons, and persons with disabilities faced heightened risks and limited recovery capacity.In response to these emergencies, the Government of Viet Nam requested continued international support to address urgent humanitarian needs while advancing early recovery and resilience-building efforts, as reflected in the 2025 Joint Response Plan (JRP) and its Addendum. These frameworks underscore the need for integrated, multi-sectoral interventions that bridge immediate relief with medium-term recovery, while reducing future disaster and climate risks.Given UNDP’s longstanding role and comparative advantage in disaster response, early recovery, and resilience building in Viet Nam, timely and coordinated intervention is essential. In close collaboration with national and provincial authorities, United Nations agencies, and development partners—including support from the Central Emergency Response Fund (CERF) and the Australian Embassy and other donors/partners—UNDP is implementing this Initiation Plan to guide emergency response and recovery interventions during 2026–2027.This Initiation Plan is aligned with UNDP’s post-disaster recovery strategy and encompasses actions across the early recovery continuum. condition.narrative: The Implementing Partner shall ensure that all of its obligations set forth under the section entitled “Legal Context and/or Risk Management Standard Clauses” are passed on to each responsible party, subcontractor and sub-recipient and that all the clauses under this section entitled “Risk Management” are included, mutatis mutandis, in all sub-contracts or sub-agreements entered into further to the Project Document
